executiveSummary.summary: Strong team with relevant experience tackling a real infrastructure gap. The co-location model is smart capital allocation, but unit economics remain unproven in low-density markets. Highway corridors offer better near-term viability than local community charging. NEVI funding complexity vs speed-to-market creates a critical strategic fork.

marketSize.tam: $100B+ US EV charging infrastructure market by 2030 (McKinsey estimate)
marketSize.sam: $15-20B rural/suburban charging market (corridors + communities outside major metros)
marketSize.som: $50-100M realistic 3-year addressable market across 5-10 target states
marketSize.methodology: Based on FHWA corridor analysis, rural EV adoption projections, and comparable charging network buildouts

competitors.name: ChargePoint
competitors.description: Largest US charging network with 65K+ stations
competitors.weakness: Focused on urban/suburban, limited rural presence

competitors.name: Electrify America
competitors.description: VW-backed network with 3,500+ DC fast chargers

competitors.name: Tesla Supercharger
competitors.description: 30K+ stations globally, opening to non-Tesla vehicles
competitors.strength: Reliability, speed, integration with Tesla ecosystem

competitors.name: Regional utilities
competitors.description: Local electric utilities building charging infrastructure
competitors.strength: Local relationships, regulatory backing, capital access

marketAnalysis.positioning: The rural charging specialist - partnering with local businesses to bring fast charging where the big networks won't go
marketAnalysis.timing: EV adoption hitting rural tipping point (15%+ of new sales in many rural counties), NEVI funding creating infrastructure push, supply chain stabilizing post-COVID

valueProposition.coreProblem: EV drivers in rural/suburban areas face 'charging deserts' - gaps of 50+ miles between fast charging options, limiting EV adoption and creating range anxiety
valueProposition.targetCustomer: Primary: Highway travelers on rural interstates. Secondary: Rural EV owners needing local fast charging
valueProposition.uniqueAngle: Asset-light co-location model reduces capex while providing revenue sharing to rural businesses, creating win-win partnerships
valueProposition.whyNow: NEVI funding mandate, EV adoption crossing 10% threshold in rural markets, charging hardware costs dropping 30% since 2020

businessModel.revenueStrategy: Per-kWh charging fees ($0.35-0.45), subscription revenue ($9.99/mo), potential demand charges and grid services revenue
businessModel.pricingFramework: $0.40/kWh average (20-30% premium to home charging, 10-15% discount to urban fast charging), subscription at $120/year for 15%+ savings

unitEconomics.estimatedCAC: $100-200 per customer (app marketing, referrals, location signage)
unitEconomics.estimatedLTV: $800-1200 over 3 years (based on 20-30 sessions/year at $25 average)
unitEconomics.paybackPeriod: 18-36 months per station depending on utilization (break-even at 15-20 sessions/day)

businessModel.scalabilityAssessment: At 10x scale (500+ stations), achieve economies in hardware procurement, operations automation, and brand recognition, but face increasing competition and site acquisition challenges

technicalFeasibility.recommendedStack: React Native app, AWS backend, Stripe payments, ChargePoint or ABB hardware integration APIs

buildVsBuy.rationale: Lease from ABB/ChargePoint - $3K/month vs $80K+ purchase, includes maintenance
Stripe + specialized EV payment providers handle compliance, PCI requirements
Differentiation opportunity, integrate real-time availability and partner locations

technicalFeasibility.estimatedMvpTimeline: 4-6 months for software platform, 6-12 months for first 5 stations operational

risks.description: Station utilization below break-even threshold (15 sessions/day) in low-traffic rural locations
risks.mitigation: Focus on highway corridors first, require traffic/demographic minimums for site selection, negotiate variable lease terms

risks.description: Tesla opens Supercharger network fully, utilities accelerate rural buildout, or oil companies deploy competing networks
risks.mitigation: Build strong local partnerships, focus on service quality and reliability, develop switching costs through subscriptions

risks.description: NEVI program requirements favor established players or change funding priorities
risks.mitigation: Diversify funding sources, build relationships with state DOTs, consider acquisition targets with NEVI compliance

risks.description: Grid capacity constraints or interconnection delays at rural locations
risks.mitigation: Partner with local utilities early, consider battery storage integration, site pre-qualification process

risks.description: Rural EV adoption slower than projected, reducing charging demand
risks.mitigation: Focus on transit corridors over local markets, track leading indicators like EV registrations, flexible site agreements

week1to4: Analyze 50+ potential highway corridor sites using traffic data and competitor mapping
Interview 20 gas station/convenience store owners about revenue-sharing interest
Model unit economics for 3 site archetypes (highway, suburban, rural town)
Begin NEVI program application research and requirements analysis
week5to8: Select and sign LOIs with 5 pilot locations on major highway corridors
Complete utility interconnection pre-applications for pilot sites
Launch beta version of mobile app with payment processing
Hire operations manager with utility/permitting experience
week9to12: Begin installation at first 2 pilot locations
Launch customer acquisition campaigns in target corridors
Submit NEVI applications if pursuing government funding
Raise Series A ($8-12M) based on pilot site performance data

decisionAgenda.question: Highway corridors vs local community charging focus?

options.tradeoff: Gain: Higher utilization, proven demand, easier customer acquisition. Lose: More competition, higher site costs, limited local relationships
Gain: Less competition, stronger local partnerships, recurring customer base. Lose: Lower utilization, slower payback, uncertain demand

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Start with highway corridors - utilization risk is lower and demand is proven. Expand to local markets once you have operational experience and cash flow.

decisionAgenda.question: Pursue NEVI funding or private capital only?

options.tradeoff: Gain: $2-5M+ in grants, regulatory support, credibility. Lose: 6-12 month delay, complex requirements, favors larger players
Gain: Speed to market, flexibility, first-mover advantage. Lose: Higher capital costs, no regulatory backing, compete against subsidized players

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Pursue both in parallel - apply for NEVI while deploying private capital for first 10-15 stations. Use early deployment success to strengthen NEVI applications.

decisionAgenda.question: Hardware ownership vs leasing strategy?

options.tradeoff: Gain: Lower upfront costs, maintenance included, easier scaling. Lose: Higher long-term costs, less control, dependency on suppliers
Gain: Lower long-term costs, full control, asset ownership. Lose: High upfront capital, maintenance responsibility, obsolescence risk

decisionAgenda.recommendation: Lease for first 25 stations to preserve capital and prove model, then evaluate purchase for subsequent buildout based on utilization data.
